# A02G Schematics I (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Feb 9, 202611:59 PM EST

A **group** submission on Gradescope.

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8) 

Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

# Learning Outcomes

* Build a digital component library using Vault components or your own custom designs.  
* Learn how to capture schematics for your custom circuit board design, leveraging the comprehensive ECAD tool, Altium.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# 

# Component Creation

In this part of the assignment, we will make sure that all the components on the BOM of A01G are available in Altium. Check to see if your components exist already in our resources. Remember, use this priority order for components:

1. Best case: UPenn ESE Vault on Altium 365, found through the **Components panel**  
2. Second best case: Manufacturer Part Search, found through the panel of the same name  
3. Third best case: Manually create component in your team’s A365 folder, as done in A01P

### Altium Starter Project

Review the Altium project starter located in the **University Of Pennsylvania (ESE) \> Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026 \> 000 Class References \>  ESE5160 S26 Starter Project ([link](https://upenn-eselabs.365.altium.com/designs/7CA1A0AE-92F4-4992-B754-F397DB9AAC1E#design))**

* Clone this project into your team folder, which has already been made for you in University Of Pennsylvania (ESE) \> Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026  
* Ensure your project is saved in the form "T\#\# Name", such as "T67 Teaching Team is Rad"  
* You will use this project for the rest of the semester. Remember that it has version control, so commit your changes frequently\! You can revert to old changes if needed.

Review the CommonComponents.SchDoc to see which components are recommended for your use.  
![][image1]

### 

### Finding Components & Recording Location

Search first in **UPenn ESE’s Altium 365 Component Vault**, accessed through the **Components panel**.  
![][image2]

If the component exists here, record the location as **UPenn ESE Vault** in your BOM Google Sheet in the Digital Component Location column.

If the component doesn’t exist there, search with **Altium’s Manufacturer Part Search (MPS) panel**.  
![][image3]  
If the component exists here, record the location as **Manufacturer Part Search** in your BOM Excel sheet in column B: Digital Component Location.

If the component doesn’t exist in UPenn ESE’s A365 Vault or Altium’s Manufacturer Part Search, you must make the part yourself. You may receive 0 points if you use SnapEDA, UltraLibrarian, or other component sources.

Make any components necessary and save them to your team’s Altium 365 folder. Review A01P, as you’ve already experienced making a component. The [Symbol Wizard](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/creating-schematic-symbol#!using_the_schematic_symbol_generation_tool) and [IPC Compliant Footprint Wizard](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/creating-pcb-footprint#!creating_a_footprint_using_the_ipc_compliant_footprint_wizard) can be a great help.

You must include 3D models for any components you make. The [IPC Compliant Footprint Wizard](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/creating-pcb-footprint#!creating_a_footprint_using_the_ipc_compliant_footprint_wizard) can make these for you. Manufacturer websites, DigiKey, and Mouser can have 3D STEP files as well\!

**It is your task to add a solution to each component.** The restrictions are: 

1. Components must be selected from **DigiKey** or **Mouser**.  
   2. Components must be **in stock** and have the **correct footprint**\!  
      1. It can be easier to look for the part number on DigiKey or Mouser, and then use that distributor part number to search for the correct BOM component in Altium.  
         1. Ex: When looking for BQ24075, Mouser’s distributor number would be **595-BQ24075TRGTT**. I would add a solution in the ActiveBOM, specifically looking for **595-BQ24075TRGTT**.  
   3. **Resistors:** Use only Stackpole Electronics Family RMCF ([Link](https://www.digikey.com/short/9rv5fjn7))  
      1. If you have special resistor requirements (tighter tolerance, size larger than 0805, larger power rating, etc.), you may expand your search on DigiKey or Mouser to find suitable MPNs.  
   4. **Capacitors:** Use only Yageo ([Link](https://www.digikey.com/short/f7cr1phv))  
      1. If you have special capacitor requirements (tighter tolerance, size larger than 0805, large capacitance value, etc.), you may expand your search on DigiKey or Mouser to find suitable MPNs.  
   5. If you are not mounting components, set them as **DNP (Do Not Populate)**. Use variants as seen in class to do this: [Design Variants | Altium Designer 25 Technical Documentation](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/design-variants)

### Important Points

* You do not need the footprint for components not soldered to your board. However, you must have connectors that go off-board to these external devices. (Hint: The CommonComponents.SchDoc has many you can use\!)  
  * Example: An OLED display needs to be mounted 2 feet away from the main PCBA, so I will use a connector and cable.  
* This question is only for the critical components you identified already in the A01G BOM assignment, such as sensors, actuators, and power supply ICs.  
  * Common passive components (1k 0603 resistors, 100nF 0603 capacitors, etc.) need not be included in this exercise.  
* Include any component you need for the power architecture. This includes any inductors, capacitors, resistors, or diodes required for buck/boost devices\!  
  * Make sure the inductors and capacitors are of appropriate size and voltage rating.  
  * There are many capacitors and resistors in the UPenn ESE Vault \- but you may need custom passive components for these converters.  
* Don’t add any new components to your A01 BOM \- unless you made dramatic changes in your project that require you to make updates.  
* You do not need to do the following parts; they will be given to you:  
  * SiWG917Y  
  * BQ24075  
  * USB to serial communication chip (FTDI)  
  * SD Card holder  
  * If you’re concerned, review the ESE5160 starter project to see what already exists.

# Schematic Capture

Complete all your project schematics in Altium. Schematics must follow the best practices discussed in class as closely as possible \- review the lecture slides to refresh your memory.

Don’t forget \- the professor has created short Altium videos for you:  [altium videos upenn ese](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/10oZzWaeaywpYi4FAVDh5ER4aY_bC2vUl?usp=sharing)

Before getting started, read through the [Schematic Design Review Checklist ESE5160 S26](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Y9rgR12MKZolKcpJ6NeAEoRgDawDYW25lLAr4cCOkWw/edit?tab=t.0) to help you achieve full credit\! Your design must pass all Tier 1 Checklist Items (Critical) to receive full credit. It behooves you to attempt as many Tier 2 Checklist Items (Less Critical) after you’ve satisfied the Tier 1 items; you’ll need to complete all Tier 2 items in the next week’s assignment.

# Tips

* We have provided screen capture videos showing you some Altium basics, including UPenn specifics. You can find them here: [altium videos upenn ese](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/10oZzWaeaywpYi4FAVDh5ER4aY_bC2vUl?usp=sharing)  
* David L. Johnes has a fantastic PCB design guide. You would do well to read it in full, multiple times throughout the process\! [David Jones PCB Design Tutorial RevA.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/19v5Q8RBW77GaVLJvEmXQC7UUIqL8F0cd/view?usp=drive_link)  
* Remember to add all source files to the folder of your project and save the project. Don’t forget to commit these libraries and schematics and push them to the cloud\!  
  * If you don’t save all files in the same project folder, you’ll see this:  
    ![][image4]  
  * If you have everything correctly saved to the server, you’ll see green check marks like:  
    ![][image5]  
* Place jumpers in series with your power supplies so it is easier to debug when we have the physical board. The following is an example:  
  ![][image6]

* Validate your project (right-click the project name and click **Validate**). Check the Messages window, which will show you the warnings and errors of your project. Go through the messages and make sure if any of them point to a warning that you must take action for (some might be false positives or very minor, “style” warnings).  

(1.1) Complete your schematics, completing at least the Tier 1 items from the Schematic Checklist.  
In the Gradescope submission, share a link to your team project in Altium 365\.

* Check to ensure the link works \- you should be able to view it in the web browser.  
* The project must contain all the schematics, schematic libraries, and PCB libraries that completely describe your circuit. Note any exceptions granted by the Teaching Team.  
* Ensure your project is saved in the form "T\#\# Name", such as "T67 Teaching Team is Rad"

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
